evals.name: empty mode asks scope
evals.prompt: A user says: improve tests. No mode or scope is provided. Describe the first response and workflow.
evals.expected_output: The response asks the user to choose a mode instead of assuming one, offering review, refactor, coverage, TDD, or full improvement, then outlines exploration and verification.
assertions: The output asks a clarifying question about what test-improvement mode to use instead of silently choosing.
The output includes mode options equivalent to review, refactor, coverage, TDD, and full.
The output says it would explore existing test files and frameworks before editing.
The output mentions running relevant test or coverage commands as part of the workflow.
The output includes verification after changes.

evals.name: refactor brittle private helper tests
evals.prompt: Mode: refactor. A Python project has many duplicate tests that call `_normalize_user_payload()` directly and assert internal mock call counts. The public behavior is `create_user(payload)`. Describe the test-improvement plan.
evals.expected_output: The response shifts tests to public behavior through create_user, avoids private helper and internal call-count coupling, consolidates duplicate cases with pytest parametrization, mocks only true system boundaries, deletes shallow tests once deeper tests cover the behavior, and verifies with pytest.
assertions: The output says the public interface create_user(payload) should be the primary test surface.
The output discourages tests coupled to the private helper _normalize_user_payload().
The output discourages asserting internal collaborator call counts as the main behavior check.
The output recommends pytest parametrization or table-style consolidation for duplicate scenarios.
The output says to mock only system boundaries rather than internal collaborators.
The output mentions deleting shallow duplicate tests once deeper behavior tests cover them.
The output includes verification with pytest or an equivalent test command.
